title: Digestive enzymes — the chemical scissors
examTip: Enzyme questions appear on every AQA Biology paper. Know each enzyme, its substrate, its products, and where it's made. pH and temperature graphs come up regularly too.
introTips: This lesson covers those proteins that are doing the cutting right now — you'll learn what each enzyme breaks down, where it works, and what it produces.
You'll also learn why enzymes stop working when the temperature or pH changes, and how to explain that in the language AQA expects.
There are only three main enzymes to learn — amylase, protease, lipase — and once you see the pattern they're hard to forget.
intro: Right now, while you're reading this, your body is cutting up molecules from your last meal into pieces small enough to absorb — and it's doing it with proteins called enzymes. They're precise, they're fast, and AQA loves testing them. Three enzymes, three substrates, three sets of products. Once you see the pattern, you'll find it very hard to forget.

keyPoints.heading: What an enzyme actually does
keyPoints.content: Think of a pair of scissors. They cut fabric faster than tearing it by hand — but the scissors aren't used up. You can use them again and again. Enzymes work the same way: they speed up a chemical reaction in your body, but come out the other side completely unchanged. That's what makes them catalysts. The part of the enzyme that actually grabs and cuts the molecule is called the active site. And here's the crucial thing about it: it has a very specific shape. Only one type of molecule fits — like a key in a lock. This is called the lock and key model.
writeBullets: Enzyme = biological catalyst (speeds up reactions without being used up)
Active site = the specific shaped region of the enzyme
Only one type of substrate fits the active site
This is called the lock and key model

keyTerms.def: A biological catalyst — a protein that speeds up chemical reactions
The specifically shaped region of an enzyme where the substrate binds
Only one substrate fits a given enzyme's active site, like a key in a lock

keyPoints.memTip: Lock = enzyme's active site. Key = substrate. Only ONE key fits ONE lock — that's why each enzyme is specific to one substrate. Sketch the lock-and-key diagram yourself and label: enzyme, active site, substrate, products.

keyPoints.heading: The three enzymes you need to know
keyPoints.content: There are three digestive enzymes AQA expects you to know cold. Amylase breaks down starch into glucose. Protease breaks down proteins into amino acids. Lipase breaks down lipids (fats and oils) into fatty acids and glycerol. Learn the pattern: enzyme → what it breaks down → what you get. That's it. The rest is detail on top of this.

keyTerms.def: Enzyme that breaks down starch into glucose
Enzyme that breaks down proteins into amino acids
Enzyme that breaks down lipids into fatty acids and glycerol

keyPoints.memTip: Draw a three-row table: Enzyme | Breaks down | Products. Fill it in from memory three times. APL — Amylase (starch → glucose), Protease (proteins → amino acids), Lipase (lipids → fatty acids + glycerol). Both products of lipase are needed for the mark.

keyPoints.heading: Where each enzyme is made
keyPoints.content: Enzymes are made in specific organs — and the pancreas is the most important one because it makes all three. Amylase is also made in your salivary glands (which is why starchy food starts tasting sweet if you chew it long enough — the amylase in your saliva is already working). Protease is also made in the stomach (the stomach version is called pepsin). All three enzymes end up working in the small intestine, where final digestion happens.
writeBullets: Pancreas makes ALL THREE enzymes (amylase, protease, lipase)
Amylase also made in salivary glands
Protease also made in stomach (stomach version = pepsin)
All three enzymes work in the small intestine

keyTerms.def: Organ that produces all three digestive enzymes (amylase, protease, lipase)
The protease made in the stomach; works at pH 2

keyPoints.memTip: The pancreas is the MVP — it's the ONLY organ that makes all three enzymes. Write 'Pancreas = A + P + L' in your notes right now. Salivary glands add amylase. Stomach adds protease (pepsin). Everything arrives in the small intestine to finish the job.

keyPoints.heading: Temperature — why 37°C is no accident
keyPoints.content: Your body temperature sits at 37°C, and that's exactly the optimum temperature for most of your enzymes — the temperature at which they work fastest. Go colder and reactions slow down (fewer collisions between enzyme and substrate). Go hotter and something permanent happens: the active site changes shape. The substrate can no longer fit. The enzyme stops working — and it can't be fixed. This is called denaturation. Note that word: not killed, not broken. Denatured. The shape is gone for good.
writeBullets: Above optimum: enzyme denatures — active site permanently changes shape
Denaturation is irreversible — cooling does NOT restore the enzyme

keyTerms.def: The temperature at which an enzyme works fastest
Permanent change in the shape of an enzyme's active site — substrate can no longer fit

keyPoints.memTip: The critical difference: below optimum = slow but recoverable. Above optimum = denatured, permanent, cannot be fixed. AQA often tests exactly this distinction. If you write 'slows down' for a denatured enzyme, you drop the mark. The word is DENATURES.

keyPoints.heading: pH — why each enzyme has its own sweet spot
keyPoints.content: Different enzymes work best at different pH levels, and your body engineers the right conditions for each one. Your salivary amylase works best at pH 7 — neutral, like the inside of your mouth. Your stomach produces hydrochloric acid to get to pH 2, which is exactly what the stomach protease (pepsin) needs. Then when food reaches the small intestine, bile (from the liver) neutralises that acid back up to around pH 7-8, so lipase and the other pancreatic enzymes can work properly. Get too far from the optimum in either direction and the active site changes shape — denaturation again.
writeBullets: Pepsin (stomach protease): optimum pH 2 — stomach produces HCl to create this
Bile neutralises stomach acid in the small intestine → pH rises to 7–8
Too far from optimum pH → active site changes shape → denaturation

keyTerms.def: The pH at which an enzyme works fastest
Substance made in the liver, stored in the gall bladder — neutralises stomach acid in the small intestine

keyPoints.memTip: Trace the pH journey food takes: mouth (pH 7, amylase works) → stomach (pH 2, pepsin works — very acidic!) → small intestine (pH 7–8, bile neutralises the acid so lipase can work). Tell this story aloud — the pH values stick better as a narrative than as a list.

1.question: What does lipase break lipids down into?
1.correctFeedback: ✅ That's it — fatty acids and glycerol. Both parts are needed for the mark.
1.wrongFeedback: Lipase works on lipids (fats). The products are fatty acids and glycerol — not glucose (that's from starch) and not amino acids (that's from protein).

3.question: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
options: It slows down temporarily and recovers when cooled
It breaks apart into amino acids
Its active site permanently changes shape so the substrate no longer fits
It moves to a different organ to keep working
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right. The active site changes shape permanently — cooling a denatured enzyme doesn't fix it.
3.wrongFeedback: Denaturation is permanent. The active site shape is gone — the substrate can't fit any more. Cooling doesn't restore it. That's the key word: permanent.

commonMistakes: Writing 'sugars' as the product of amylase — AQA wants 'glucose'. Be specific.
Saying enzymes are 'killed' by heat — they're denatured. They're not alive, they can't be killed. Use the right word.

The pancreas produces all three enzymes: amylase (breaks down starch), protease (breaks down proteins) and lipase (breaks down lipids). It is the only organ that makes all three. If an exam question asks which organ is most important for enzyme production, the answer is the pancreas.
Mixing up substrate and product — the substrate is what goes in, the product is what comes out.
examTips: For any enzyme question: give the substrate AND the product. One without the other usually drops a mark.
For denaturation: you need two things — the active site 'changes shape' AND the substrate 'can no longer fit'. Both parts, both marks.
On graph questions: the rate rises to a peak (the optimum), then drops sharply as denaturation kicks in above it. The drop is sudden, not gradual.
revisionCardBullets: Enzyme = biological catalyst (a protein) — speeds up reactions without being used up
Active site = the specifically shaped region of an enzyme — only one substrate fits (lock and key model)
Amylase breaks down starch → glucose | made in salivary glands and pancreas
Protease breaks down proteins → amino acids | made in stomach (pepsin) and pancreas
Lipase breaks down lipids → fatty acids + glycerol | made in pancreas
The pancreas makes all three enzymes — it's the most important enzyme-producing organ
Optimum temperature for human enzymes ≈ 37°C — above this, denaturation occurs
Denaturation = active site permanently changes shape — substrate can no longer fit — irreversible
Salivary amylase works best at pH 7 (neutral — mouth)
Pepsin (stomach protease) works best at pH 2 — stomach produces HCl to create this
Bile neutralises stomach acid in the small intestine, raising pH so lipase and other enzymes can work
